items.rationale: Crypto seen as the counterbalance to centralized AI: permissionless global markets for compute/data drive down AI costs, and blockchains are the only credible way to prove origin of content in a deepfake-saturated internet.
items.age_note: Emerging theme 2022 ('machines can think on their own'), intensified through 2023 into Jan 2024; current and rising

items.rationale: Treated as the single defining property that changes risk profile, enables permissionless innovation, fixes web2 holdup, and is the basis for regulatory defensibility; team believes best practices have matured enough for a 'new era.'

Viewed as possibly the most powerful crypto primitive of the decade — unlocks privacy, scaling and programmability; prover speed breakthroughs (Lasso/Jolt) and a VERIFY_SNARK opcode could make even Bitcoin programmable.

Considered a prerequisite for mainstream adoption; team funds encrypted-Ethereum infra (Aztec) and argues privacy must be paired with fine-grained, provable-compliance regulation rather than blanket sanctions.

L2s prized less for raw performance than for enabling permissionless architectural innovation atop Ethereum; danksharding/DAS expected to make rollups dramatically cheaper and unlock new applications.
Web3 unlocks community-built IP and 'multiplayer for IP'; long-run collectibles business modeled on Pokémon-style abundance rather than ultra-scarcity, with creators compensated for downstream reuse.
items.age_note: Strong 2022 (CC0 / Can't Be Evil licenses) sustained into 2023 (Story Protocol); current

items.rationale: Composability, interoperability and true player ownership of assets seen as enabling modding-as-foreground and player-built games on shared on-chain contracts.

Web2 platforms suffer from holdup and arbitrary deplatforming; portable follow-graphs and protocol-based social create credible commitments, but require decentralized names/value/data to resist re-centralization.
Blockchains as 'open-source services'; viability gated by a simple economic test (verification cost below incumbent margin), with large idle physical capacity to tap.

Multi-chain future treated as settled; interop infra (LayerZero) that decouples security from liveness, plus shared sequencers, seen as critical rails — though shared sequencing raises winner-take-all/MEV questions.

Tokens valued as alignment/governance tools and 'value vectors,' not equity or fundraising vehicles; recent push toward developer-incentive and B2B models and away from user-acquisition/speculation designs.
items.age_note: ICO-as-fundraising explicitly rejected since 2017–2018; reaffirmed Jan 2024 with B2B tilt

items.rationale: Cheap, fast USDC transfers can cut ~30% merchant fees and preserve direct customer relationships — the clearest near-term consumer benefit.

Seen as the dominant existential threat: deliberately fostered uncertainty, refusal to give guidance since 2019, and an unprecedented extension of Howey to secondary markets/exchanges intended to ban or offshore the technology.
items.age_note: Escalated from 'regulation-by-enforcement has failed' (2022) to 'endgame is banning the technology' (2023) and SEC v. Coinbase chokepoint analysis (Jan 2024); most recent and most intense

items.rationale: Viewed as the primary force holding the space back and as cover for bad behavior; team explicitly rejects the 'need the casino to bootstrap' argument and pushes toward purposeful, long-term-aligned mechanisms.
items.age_note: Sharpest articulation Dec 2023; consistent with 2023 'tired vs underexplored' framing

items.rationale: FTX/Celsius/Voyager-type failures attributed to trust, not crypto; custody is a liability that keeps getting hacked, and 'trust must be regulated differently than trustless.'

Front-running/reordering/sniping treated as a large, ongoing tax on users; team works on mitigations (faster blocks, order-flow auctions, PROF) but considers it an unsolved structural problem, worsened in cross-chain settings.
items.age_note: Went from 'fringe' to center of protocol design (noted 2022); still unresolved as of late 2023

items.rationale: Research shows flight-to-safety across stablecoins driven by varied risks (reserve quality, regulatory, counterparty), with liquidity providers running first and amplifying run risk on 'less safe' coins — bad collateral, not algorithms per se, is the root cause.
items.age_note: Core 'bad collateral not algorithms' thesis from 2022 op-eds; empirically reinforced by Sept 2023 research

items.rationale: Smart-contract development described as the opposite of agile and not yet safe to rely on; ZK circuit bugs are especially hard because only the proof is shared, hiding the malicious input.
Proliferating chains/rollups split security and liquidity, raising systemic risk (51% attacks, over-leveraged restaking quorums); modularity unlocks innovation but locks in hard-to-change interfaces.
Fixed (non-dilutable) token allocations in equity deals seen as transferring value from founders to investors, removing follow-on incentives and forcing premature token launches.

Direct democracy doesn't scale and invites capture by small motivated groups; token-weighted plutocracy lacks legitimacy for public-good systems — team favors indirect/representative models and accountable ('Machiavellian') DAOs.
items.age_note: Identified 2022; remedy refined into 'Machiavellian DAOs' by Dec 2023

items.rationale: A future quantum computer could forge signatures, fork PoS consensus, and advantage quantum PoW miners; defenses exist but post-quantum signatures bloat blockchain state — roughly a 15-year watch item.

Regarded as the standout developer-led ecosystem and high-economic-security settlement layer for a nation-state-scale economy; SEC's approval of ETH futures read as implicit concession that ETH is a commodity, not a security.

Seen as technically a better gold via cryptographic scarcity and most-battle-tested security; excitement is forward-looking about adding ZK/programmability (VERIFY_SNARK opcode) — tempered by long-run security-budget and quantum concerns.
items.age_note: Long-held store-of-value view; 2023 shift toward wanting ZK programmability on Bitcoin

items.rationale: Treated as comparatively transparent and 'likely okay' under securities law (not its own issuer's investment scheme), and the rail behind cheap merchant payments; a flight-to-safety beneficiary in some stress episodes.
Exogenous collateral makes DAI structurally safer than endogenous designs like Terra; MakerDAO repeatedly cited as the case where token-based governance actually works.
items.age_note: Defended 2022; governance praise restated 2022–2023, not heavily restated in 2024

items.rationale: Endogenous-collateral designs depend on market belief and can collapse into death spirals; UST also cited as the clean Howey case (issuer + Anchor's unsustainable 20% yield). Nuance: team blames bad collateral, not algorithms per se.
items.age_note: Concern constant since 2022; 'bad collateral not algorithms' framing persists into the Dec 2023 Terraform ruling commentary

items.rationale: Opaque reserve quality drives repeated concern that it could break the peg under stress — even though regulatory-arbitrage flows ironically pile into it during US-centric shocks.
Single entity controlling >1/3 of staked ETH raises centralization/concentration risk for Ethereum consensus; team notes opinion is split but flags it as a parameter users should manage.
Requiring users to hold each chain's native token for fees fragments liquidity and adds UX friction ('Cosmos problem'); seen as a drag on adoption.

risk_regime.rationale: The house is structurally, high-conviction bullish on the technology and the long-term decentralization opportunity (AI×crypto, ZK, privacy, L2 scaling, tokens-as-coordination), and counsels founders to 'stay the course' through the bear market and regulatory pressure. Crucially, this is 'builder-bullish, casino-bearish': the team is deliberately risk-OFF on speculation, leverage, memecoins and mercenary token designs, and tactically defensive about US regulation (SEC) and CeFi/custodial risk. So the posture is conviction in building/structural exposure, paired with explicit rejection of speculative risk-taking.

notes: Corpus is dominated by a few distinct voices (a legal/policy voice on SEC/decentralization; a research voice on consensus/ZK/MEV/economics; an investing/thesis voice on AI×crypto and network design; a market-design voice on NFTs/auctions/governance), so 'consensus' here means the convergent house themes, not unanimous agreement on every detail. Recency weighting: regulatory hostility, anti-casino stance, AI×crypto, decentralization-as-maturing-practice, and predatory-token-allocation warnings are the freshest (late-2023→Jan-2024) and weighted most. Notable nuance/reversals captured in age_notes: stablecoin view is consistently 'bad collateral, not algorithms'; ICO/token-as-fundraising was embraced ~2017 then firmly rejected from 2018 onward; ETH-is-a-security risk is treated as effectively foreclosed after the Oct-2023 futures approval. Two team-level skeptics-turned-believers (originally 'crypto skeptic' on decentralizing already-efficient services) now frame value around composability, embeddedness, and community cohesion. MakerDAO/DAI and quantum sit closer to 'persisted' — held but not heavily restated in the most recent window.
